texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theironandsteelindustryconsumeshugeamountoffossilfuels and discharges carbon dioxide (CO2) as well as power industry. According to the recent survey (GIO, 2009), Japan's total CO2 emission in 2007 was about 1.2 billion tons. The largest emission source was power generation (34%) and the second was iron and steel industry (13%). That is, following the power industry, the iron and steel industry is one of the highest targets to reduce greenhouse gas emission.
texts.text: Theironandsteelindustryconsumeshugeamountoffossilfuels and discharges carbon dioxide (CO2) as well as power industry. According to the recent survey (GIO, 2009), Japan's total CO2 emission in 2007 was about 1.2 billion tons. The largest emission source was power generation (34%) and the second was iron and steel industry (13%). That is, following the power industry, the iron and steel industry is one of the highest targets to reduce greenhouse gas emission.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 capture is a key technology of CO2 capture and storage (CCS), which is regarded as a promising potential for tackling global warming. Also CO2 capture is important from the viewpoint of cost reduction because it covers large portion of the total CCS cost. In the recent studies, many researches on aqueous amine solvents for CO2 capture were carried out in the field of a fundamental research and post-combustion CO2 capture. For example, the relationship between absorption/desorption rates and amine structure was investigated using screening tests (Bonenfant et al., 2003). Laboratory-scale experiments were conducted on amine solvent vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) and enthalpy of absorption (Jou et al., 1994; Mathonat et al., 1998; Mimura et al., 2005). Hindered amine solvents were shown to have good desorption behavior and
texts.text: CO2 capture is a key technology of CO2 capture and storage (CCS), which is regarded as a promising potential for tackling global warming. Also CO2 capture is important from the viewpoint of cost reduction because it covers large portion of the total CCS cost. In the recent studies, many researches on aqueous amine solvents for CO2 capture were carried out in the field of a fundamental research and post-combustion CO2 capture. For example, the relationship between absorption/desorption rates and amine structure was investigated using screening tests (Bonenfant et al., 2003). Laboratory-scale experiments were conducted on amine solvent vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) and enthalpy of absorption (Jou et al., 1994; Mathonat et al., 1998; Mimura et al., 2005). Hindered amine solvents were shown to have good desorption behavior and

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to establish energy-saving technology for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas, novel absorbents weredevelopedinthelaboratoryandevaluatedata1tCO2/dtestplant.Atfirst,CO2 absorptionanddesorption behaviors of single-component amine solvents for simulated blast furnace gas (CO2/N2 = 20%/80%) were investigated through a screening test using a small scrubbing bottle. These amine solvents were additionally analyzed using nuclear magnetic resonance ( 13 C NMR) spectroscopy and reaction calorimetry. The results of the laboratory experiments showed that there was a trade-off between absorption rate and enthalpy of absorption but some absorbents had unique features. For example, 2isopropylaminoethanol (IPAE) had high absorption rate and small enthalpy of absorption. Then, new IPAE-based amine solvents (RITE solvents: RITE-A and RITE-B) were formulated and evaluated at the 1tCO2/d test plant. CO2 regeneration energies of the RITE solvents were 3.3 and 3.1 GJ/tCO2, respectively. With certain process conditions and plant specifications optimized, RITE-B was estimated to have the potential to achieve 2.5 GJ/tCO2.
texts.text: In order to establish energy-saving technology for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas, novel absorbents weredevelopedinthelaboratoryandevaluatedata1tCO2/dtestplant.Atfirst,CO2 absorptionanddesorption behaviors of single-component amine solvents for simulated blast furnace gas (CO2/N2 = 20%/80%) were investigated through a screening test using a small scrubbing bottle. These amine solvents were additionally analyzed using nuclear magnetic resonance ( 13 C NMR) spectroscopy and reaction calorimetry. The results of the laboratory experiments showed that there was a trade-off between absorption rate and enthalpy of absorption but some absorbents had unique features. For example, 2isopropylaminoethanol (IPAE) had high absorption rate and small enthalpy of absorption. Then, new IPAE-based amine solvents (RITE solvents: RITE-A and RITE-B) were formulated and evaluated at the 1tCO2/d test plant. CO2 regeneration energies of the RITE solvents were 3.3 and 3.1 GJ/tCO2, respectively. With certain process conditions and plant specifications optimized, RITE-B was estimated to have the potential to achieve 2.5 GJ/tCO2.

They developed potential candidate solvents from their results in the laboratory, and highlighted the necessity for scaledup experiments to evaluate these high performance absorbents.

They developed potential candidate solvents from their results in the laboratory, and highlighted the necessity for scaledup experiments to evaluate these high performance absorbents.

texts.orig: However, there were few researches for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas in the iron-making process except for the followings. Tobiesen et al. (2007) applied a model analysis to estimate the performanceofchemicalabsorptionforCO2 captureformblastfurnace gas. Lie et al. (2007) investigated membrane process for CO2 capture in the steelmaking industry.
texts.text: However, there were few researches for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas in the iron-making process except for the followings. Tobiesen et al. (2007) applied a model analysis to estimate the performanceofchemicalabsorptionforCO2 captureformblastfurnace gas. Lie et al. (2007) investigated membrane process for CO2 capture in the steelmaking industry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: RITE (Research Institute of Innovative Technology for the Earth) developed novel absorbents for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas in integrated steelworks. The COCS project (Cost-Saving CO2 capture System, 2004-2008) was set up in 2004, in order to develop a low-cost CO2 capture system (Shimizu et al., 2006). The concept of the project was to remove CO2 from blast furnace gas using chemical absorption (Fig. 1). The goal of the project was to reduce the CO2 capture costs by half. One of the research objectives was to develop high-performance absorbents for a CO2 scrubbing solvent so as to capture CO2 using less energy. RITE focused on the blast furnace gas which was processed at the facilities such as blast furnace top gas pressure recovery power generation and dust removal. The condition of the blast furnace gas investigated in the COCS project was

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, aqueous amine solvents were investigated to develop high-performance solvents for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas. Firstly, a screening test using a scrubbing bottle to obtain absorption and desorption behaviors for single-amine solvents. In addition, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy ( 13 C NMR) and reaction calorimetry were carried out. Then, blended amine solvents were formulated on the basis of experimental results of the single-amine solvents. They were also examined in the laboratory and evaluated at a 1 tCO2 /d test plant.
texts.text: In this study, aqueous amine solvents were investigated to develop high-performance solvents for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas. Firstly, a screening test using a scrubbing bottle to obtain absorption and desorption behaviors for single-amine solvents. In addition, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy ( 13 C NMR) and reaction calorimetry were carried out. Then, blended amine solvents were formulated on the basis of experimental results of the single-amine solvents. They were also examined in the laboratory and evaluated at a 1 tCO2 /d test plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aminesinvestigated in this paper are listed in Table 1. All singlecomponent amines, except for 2-isopropylaminoethanol (IPAE), were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. and Tokyo Kasei KogyoCo.Ltd.andwereusedwithoutfurtherpurification.IPAEwas prepared using a hydroxyalkylation reaction between isopropylamine and 2-bromoethanol. After rectification, the purity of the synthesized IPAE was established by gas chromatography and mass spectroscopy. For all single-component amines except for piperazine (PZ), a 30 wt% solution was used in the 13 C NMR, reaction calorimetry and screening tests. The PZ solution was made up to 2mol/L. RITE solvents were IPAE-based mixtures of amines and formulated on the basis of experimental results mentioned later

After rectification, the purity of the synthesized IPAE was established by gas chromatography and mass spectroscopy. For all single-component amines except for piperazine (PZ), a 30 wt% solution was used in the 13 C NMR, reaction calorimetry and screening tests. The PZ solution was made up to 2mol/L. RITE solvents were IPAE-based mixtures of amines and formulated on the basis of experimental results mentioned later

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (2) Screening test
texts.text: (2) Screening test

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Screening tests were conducted as an initial attempt to investigate absorption and desorption behaviors of a solvent. A 250 ml glass scrubbing-bottle containing 50ml of amine solvent was placed in a water bath of 40 ◦ C. A gas mixture (CO2/N2 = 20/80%) was then supplied to the bottle at a flow rate of 700 ml/min. After 60min of CO2 absorption, the bottle was moved to a water bath of 70 ◦ CandCO2 wasdesorbedfromthesolvent.TheflowrateandCO2 concentration of the feed gas were constant during the absorption and desorption tests. The outlet gas from the bottle was analyzed with a carbon dioxide analyzer (VA-3001, Horiba). A curve of CO2 loading with time was estimated from the measured CO2 concentration of outlet gas flow. A gradient of the curve at a half point of the 60-min CO2 loading was calculated as an absorption rate. The curve of CO2 loading also showed absorption amounts of CO2 at 40 ◦ C and 70 ◦ C.
texts.text: Screening tests were conducted as an initial attempt to investigate absorption and desorption behaviors of a solvent. A 250 ml glass scrubbing-bottle containing 50ml of amine solvent was placed in a water bath of 40 ◦ C. A gas mixture (CO2/N2 = 20/80%) was then supplied to the bottle at a flow rate of 700 ml/min. After 60min of CO2 absorption, the bottle was moved to a water bath of 70 ◦ CandCO2 wasdesorbedfromthesolvent.TheflowrateandCO2 concentration of the feed gas were constant during the absorption and desorption tests. The outlet gas from the bottle was analyzed with a carbon dioxide analyzer (VA-3001, Horiba). A curve of CO2 loading with time was estimated from the measured CO2 concentration of outlet gas flow. A gradient of the curve at a half point of the 60-min CO2 loading was calculated as an absorption rate. The curve of CO2 loading also showed absorption amounts of CO2 at 40 ◦ C and 70 ◦ C.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (3) 13 C NMR spectroscopy
texts.text: (3) 13 C NMR spectroscopy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 13 C NMR spectroscopy was used to analyze the CO2-absorbed amine solvents and to determine the ratios of carbamate and bicarbonate anions produced. As mentioned in the report (IEA GHG, 2007), CO2 absorption by bicarbonate anion has an advantage of grater absorption capacity than CO2 absorption by a carbamate anion because bicarbonate anion is formed from one mole of CO2 and one mole of amine in a reaction where water acts as a homogeneous catalyst.
texts.text: 13 C NMR spectroscopy was used to analyze the CO2-absorbed amine solvents and to determine the ratios of carbamate and bicarbonate anions produced. As mentioned in the report (IEA GHG, 2007), CO2 absorption by bicarbonate anion has an advantage of grater absorption capacity than CO2 absorption by a carbamate anion because bicarbonate anion is formed from one mole of CO2 and one mole of amine in a reaction where water acts as a homogeneous catalyst.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The measurements were performed at 298-299K in aqueous solutions of single-component amines using a JEOL JNM-ECA400 spectrometer. Before analyzing a solvent, 20%-CO2 gas was injected into the solvent for 60 min at 40 ◦ C. This pretreatment was the same as that used in the screening tests. Quantitaive spectra were obtained with a delay of 30 s, a pulse width of 9 𝛍 s, and 400 scans. The measurement frequency was 100MHz.
texts.text: The measurements were performed at 298-299K in aqueous solutions of single-component amines using a JEOL JNM-ECA400 spectrometer. Before analyzing a solvent, 20%-CO2 gas was injected into the solvent for 60 min at 40 ◦ C. This pretreatment was the same as that used in the screening tests. Quantitaive spectra were obtained with a delay of 30 s, a pulse width of 9 𝛍 s, and 400 scans. The measurement frequency was 100MHz.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The enthalpy change during CO2 absorption was measured by reaction calorimetry as Mimura et al. (1995) did. They developed new energy-saving solvents with a feature of small enthalpy of absorption. In our measurement, there were two assumptions.
texts.text: The enthalpy change during CO2 absorption was measured by reaction calorimetry as Mimura et al. (1995) did. They developed new energy-saving solvents with a feature of small enthalpy of absorption. In our measurement, there were two assumptions.

texts.orig: First, that CO2 absorption involves multiple electrolyte reactions in a liquid phase but only the overall reaction was considered. The secondwasthatCO2 absorption and desorption are reversible reactions with equal absolute values. A differential reaction calorimeter (DRC, SETARAM)wasusedtomeasurethetotalheatgeneratedduring CO2 absorption. The measurement was conducted at 40 ◦ C in temperature. The volume of a solvent used was 150 ml and CO2 was injected at a partial pressure 0.1 MPa with CO2 flow of 120 mL/min at a room temperature. Sensible heat of the injected CO2 was small and was negligible.
texts.text: First, that CO2 absorption involves multiple electrolyte reactions in a liquid phase but only the overall reaction was considered. The secondwasthatCO2 absorption and desorption are reversible reactions with equal absolute values. A differential reaction calorimeter (DRC, SETARAM)wasusedtomeasurethetotalheatgeneratedduring CO2 absorption. The measurement was conducted at 40 ◦ C in temperature. The volume of a solvent used was 150 ml and CO2 was injected at a partial pressure 0.1 MPa with CO2 flow of 120 mL/min at a room temperature. Sensible heat of the injected CO2 was small and was negligible.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amount of absorbed CO2 in a solvent was measured by a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-VCH, Shimadzu). The enthalpy of absorption was calculated by dividing the total heat generated during absorption by the amount of CO2 increase of the solvent.
texts.text: The amount of absorbed CO2 in a solvent was measured by a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-VCH, Shimadzu). The enthalpy of absorption was calculated by dividing the total heat generated during absorption by the amount of CO2 increase of the solvent.

texts.orig: 2.2. 1tCO2/d plant test
texts.text: 2.2. 1tCO2/d plant test

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Plant tests were conducted in order to evaluate CO2 regenerationenergyofthesolventsinthisstudy.Atestplantfacilityinstalled at the Kimitsu works of Nippon Steel Co. (Fig. 2) had a capacity to feed a blast furnace gas of 40-120 N m 3 /h (maximum 1 t/d). It was operated using blast furnace gas supplied from the commercially operated iron-making facilities. The blast furnace gas was a reducing gas and 20% CO2 contained. The plant had one absorber column (packing height 4.3 m, diameter 0.27 m) and one stripper column (packing height 2.1 m, diameter 0.20 m). Both the absorber and the stripper columns were packed with the structured FLEXIPAC 1Y HC (Koch Glitsch). The reboiler was heated using factory steam from the steelworks and controlled at approximately 120 ◦ C to regener-

texts.orig: In order to evaluate the solvent performance excluding the effect of absorber specification, a wide range of research operations were carried out to find minimum CO2 regeneration energy. That is, in order to increase CO2 loading of rich solvent, liquidgas ratio and gas feed rate was changed. Decrease in the gas feed rate had an effect on increase in gas-liquid contact time in the absorber.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Characteristics of aqueous amine solvents
texts.text: 3.1. Characteristics of aqueous amine solvents

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (1) Absorption rate and maximum CO2 loading
texts.text: (1) Absorption rate and maximum CO2 loading

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The screening test provided relative absorption/desorption rates, CO2 loadings at 40 ◦ C and 70 ◦ C (Fig. 3 and Table 2). The results show high absorption rates for 2-aminoethanol (MEA), 2-ethylaminoethanol (EAE) and piperazine (PZ). 2Isopropylaminoethanol (IPAE) was moderately-high absorption rate. 2-Amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) and Diethanolamine (DEA) were slightly low absorption rate. Methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) absorbed CO2 very slowly and did not reach saturation after 60 min. The maximum absorption amount at 40 ◦ C followed the order of molecular weight because the solvent used in this screening test were prepared at the same concentration.
texts.text: The screening test provided relative absorption/desorption rates, CO2 loadings at 40 ◦ C and 70 ◦ C (Fig. 3 and Table 2). The results show high absorption rates for 2-aminoethanol (MEA), 2-ethylaminoethanol (EAE) and piperazine (PZ). 2Isopropylaminoethanol (IPAE) was moderately-high absorption rate. 2-Amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) and Diethanolamine (DEA) were slightly low absorption rate. Methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) absorbed CO2 very slowly and did not reach saturation after 60 min. The maximum absorption amount at 40 ◦ C followed the order of molecular weight because the solvent used in this screening test were prepared at the same concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The RITE solvents were formulated with regard to high concentration as well as amine component. Compared with singlecomponent amine solvents, the RITE solvents showed much higher CO2 loading at 40 ◦ C and the moderately high absorption rate.
texts.text: The RITE solvents were formulated with regard to high concentration as well as amine component. Compared with singlecomponent amine solvents, the RITE solvents showed much higher CO2 loading at 40 ◦ C and the moderately high absorption rate.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (2) CO2 absorption mechanism
texts.text: (2) CO2 absorption mechanism

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both carbamate and bicarbonate anions in a CO2-absorbed solvent were quantitatively analyzed from the 13 C NMR spectra of absorbed CO2 and used to calculate the ratio of these two anions. Fig. 4 shows the results of 13 C NMR analysis. MEA mainly absorbed CO2 as carbamate anion and ratio of the carbamate anion to total absorbed CO2 was 82%. This result was in good agreement with 84% reported in the previous research (Böttinger et al., 2008). MEA and AMP are both classified as primary amines but they had different CO2 absorption mechanism. AMP mainly absorbed CO2 as bicarbonate anion. The structure of AMP contains steric hindrance and CO2 was mostly absorbed as bicarbonate anions.
texts.text: Both carbamate and bicarbonate anions in a CO2-absorbed solvent were quantitatively analyzed from the 13 C NMR spectra of absorbed CO2 and used to calculate the ratio of these two anions. Fig. 4 shows the results of 13 C NMR analysis. MEA mainly absorbed CO2 as carbamate anion and ratio of the carbamate anion to total absorbed CO2 was 82%. This result was in good agreement with 84% reported in the previous research (Böttinger et al., 2008). MEA and AMP are both classified as primary amines but they had different CO2 absorption mechanism. AMP mainly absorbed CO2 as bicarbonate anion. The structure of AMP contains steric hindrance and CO2 was mostly absorbed as bicarbonate anions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DEA, EAE and IPAE are secondary amines and they all absorbed CO2 as both carbamate and bicarbonate anions. The bicarbonate anion ratio followed the order DEA<EAE<IPAE. This order also represented capacity of CO2 absorption. IPAE could react with CO2 close to 1 molCO2 /mol amine . The RITE solvents were IPAE based mixtures and derived this feature from a single IPAE solvent. MDEA is a tertiary amine and CO2 was completely absorbed as bicarbonate anion. PZ could be classified into the secondary amine but its result was similar to that of MEA.
texts.text: DEA, EAE and IPAE are secondary amines and they all absorbed CO2 as both carbamate and bicarbonate anions. The bicarbonate anion ratio followed the order DEA<EAE<IPAE. This order also represented capacity of CO2 absorption. IPAE could react with CO2 close to 1 molCO2 /mol amine . The RITE solvents were IPAE based mixtures and derived this feature from a single IPAE solvent. MDEA is a tertiary amine and CO2 was completely absorbed as bicarbonate anion. PZ could be classified into the secondary amine but its result was similar to that of MEA.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Experimental results at 1 tCO2 /d plant
texts.text: 3.2. Experimental results at 1 tCO2 /d plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 3 shows the results of plant experiments in which the lowest regeneration energy was demonstrated. The new absorbents, RITE-A and RITE-B, showed low lean loading and high rich loading with large cyclic capacity and smaller liquid-to-gas ratios than MEA30wt%.It showed that the feature of large absorption amount shown in the screening test was reflected on the reduction of solvent circulation rate. As a result, the regeneration energies were 3.3 GJ/tCO2 for RITE-A and 3.1 GJ/tCO2 for RITE-B.
texts.text: Table 3 shows the results of plant experiments in which the lowest regeneration energy was demonstrated. The new absorbents, RITE-A and RITE-B, showed low lean loading and high rich loading with large cyclic capacity and smaller liquid-to-gas ratios than MEA30wt%.It showed that the feature of large absorption amount shown in the screening test was reflected on the reduction of solvent circulation rate. As a result, the regeneration energies were 3.3 GJ/tCO2 for RITE-A and 3.1 GJ/tCO2 for RITE-B.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, the amount of CO2 production followed the order MEA>RITE-A>RITE-B. This order was same as that of CO2 absorption rate shown in Table 2. That is, absorber's specification seemed to be insufficient for the RITE solvents.
texts.text: However, the amount of CO2 production followed the order MEA>RITE-A>RITE-B. This order was same as that of CO2 absorption rate shown in Table 2. That is, absorber's specification seemed to be insufficient for the RITE solvents.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Discussion
texts.text: 4. Discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Absorbent selection and formulation
texts.text: 4.1. Absorbent selection and formulation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a result of laboratory experiments, the relation between enthalpy of absorption and absorption rate were plotted in Fig. 6. It shows that there was a trade-off relationship between enthalpy of absorption and absorption rate for primary, secondary and tertiary amines. That is, absorbents with a low enthalpy of absorption usually had a low CO2 absorption rate. However, novel absorbent should be plotted at the area of low enthalpy of absorption and high CO2 absorption rate.
texts.text: As a result of laboratory experiments, the relation between enthalpy of absorption and absorption rate were plotted in Fig. 6. It shows that there was a trade-off relationship between enthalpy of absorption and absorption rate for primary, secondary and tertiary amines. That is, absorbents with a low enthalpy of absorption usually had a low CO2 absorption rate. However, novel absorbent should be plotted at the area of low enthalpy of absorption and high CO2 absorption rate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study the IPAE and RITE solvents were plotted outside the area formed by the other single-component amine solvents that followed this relationship. This indicates that IPAE and the RITE solvents have unique performance in that they feature a low enthalpyofabsorptionbutretainamoderatelyhighCO2 absorption rate.
texts.text: In this study the IPAE and RITE solvents were plotted outside the area formed by the other single-component amine solvents that followed this relationship. This indicates that IPAE and the RITE solvents have unique performance in that they feature a low enthalpyofabsorptionbutretainamoderatelyhighCO2 absorption rate.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Performance of RITE solvents
texts.text: 4.2. Performance of RITE solvents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 regeneration energy is sum of four terms: enthalpy of desorption ( Qdes ), solvent heating ( Qsol ), steam stripping ( Qsteam ) and heat loss from facility ( Qloss ). First three terms are described in the previous research (Hoff et al., 2006). Qloss can be obtained by subtracting the three terms from the experimental result of CO2 regeneration energy.
texts.text: CO2 regeneration energy is sum of four terms: enthalpy of desorption ( Qdes ), solvent heating ( Qsol ), steam stripping ( Qsteam ) and heat loss from facility ( Qloss ). First three terms are described in the previous research (Hoff et al., 2006). Qloss can be obtained by subtracting the three terms from the experimental result of CO2 regeneration energy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to evaluate the RITE solvents, CO2 regeneration energy obtainedattestplantwasmathematicallyanalyzedfromviewpoint of the above-mentioned heat balance. The measured enthalpy of absorption can be used for the enthalpy of desorption. Process variables such as material flow, temperature, pressure and so on, can be gathered from the plant test data. Specific heat of solvent can be determined from amine concentration and component's property.
texts.text: In order to evaluate the RITE solvents, CO2 regeneration energy obtainedattestplantwasmathematicallyanalyzedfromviewpoint of the above-mentioned heat balance. The measured enthalpy of absorption can be used for the enthalpy of desorption. Process variables such as material flow, temperature, pressure and so on, can be gathered from the plant test data. Specific heat of solvent can be determined from amine concentration and component's property.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the results of total CO2 regeneration energy and four terms for RITE-A and RITE-B. It showed that the solvent heating and heat loss in the experimental results of RITE-A and RITE-B were large because of a small plant size and a poor plant-design. Theninordertoestimate regeneration energy for each RITE solvent under optimal conditions, following two assumptions were made. First, plant specifications were assumed to be optimized to give a rich-lean solvent temperature difference at the heat exchanger of 10 ◦ C. Second, it was assumed that the 'heat loss from facility' could bereduced,to10%oftotalregenerationenergy,incommercialplant operations due to scale effect.
texts.text: Fig. 7 shows the results of total CO2 regeneration energy and four terms for RITE-A and RITE-B. It showed that the solvent heating and heat loss in the experimental results of RITE-A and RITE-B were large because of a small plant size and a poor plant-design. Theninordertoestimate regeneration energy for each RITE solvent under optimal conditions, following two assumptions were made. First, plant specifications were assumed to be optimized to give a rich-lean solvent temperature difference at the heat exchanger of 10 ◦ C. Second, it was assumed that the 'heat loss from facility' could bereduced,to10%oftotalregenerationenergy,incommercialplant operations due to scale effect.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the basis of those assumptions, the energy of solvent heating was calculated to be 0.3 GJ/tCO2 and heat loss form facility was calculated to be 0.2 GJ/tCO2 for both RITE solvents. As a result, the estimated regeneration energies from this heat balance analysis were 2.7GJ/tCO2 for RITE-A and 2.5 GJ/tCO2 for RITE-B.
texts.text: On the basis of those assumptions, the energy of solvent heating was calculated to be 0.3 GJ/tCO2 and heat loss form facility was calculated to be 0.2 GJ/tCO2 for both RITE solvents. As a result, the estimated regeneration energies from this heat balance analysis were 2.7GJ/tCO2 for RITE-A and 2.5 GJ/tCO2 for RITE-B.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: RITE developed novel absorbents for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas to establish a energy-saving CO2 capture system for integrated steelworks. Amines with low enthalpy of absorption and high absorption rate were investigated by screening test, 13 C NMR analysis and reaction calorimetry. New blended amine solvents were formulated and examined both in the laboratory and at a 1 t CO2 /d test plant. The results were as follows:
texts.text: RITE developed novel absorbents for CO2 capture from blast furnace gas to establish a energy-saving CO2 capture system for integrated steelworks. Amines with low enthalpy of absorption and high absorption rate were investigated by screening test, 13 C NMR analysis and reaction calorimetry. New blended amine solvents were formulated and examined both in the laboratory and at a 1 t CO2 /d test plant. The results were as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) Through the laboratory experiments, it showed that the secondary amine IPAE mainly generated bicarbonate anions and had a high absorption capacity. Also IPAE showed small enthalpy of desorption compared with the other amines within their chemical group (primary or secondary amines). In addition, IPAE had better features both of enthalpy of absorption and CO2 absorption rate, compared with the amine solvents typically employed which all showed the trade-off relationship.
texts.text: Through the laboratory experiments, it showed that the secondary amine IPAE mainly generated bicarbonate anions and had a high absorption capacity. Also IPAE showed small enthalpy of desorption compared with the other amines within their chemical group (primary or secondary amines). In addition, IPAE had better features both of enthalpy of absorption and CO2 absorption rate, compared with the amine solvents typically employed which all showed the trade-off relationship.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) RITE solvents were developed with IPAE in this study. The RITE solvents showed better performance in the laboratory experiments. They had features of small enthalpy of absorption and a moderately high absorption rate.
texts.text: RITE solvents were developed with IPAE in this study. The RITE solvents showed better performance in the laboratory experiments. They had features of small enthalpy of absorption and a moderately high absorption rate.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) The RITE solvents were evaluated at the 1tCO2/d test plant. Regeneration energies were 3.3 GJ/tCO2 and 3.1 GJ/tCO2 for RITEA and RITE-B, respectively. Furthermore, after optimization for the RITE solvents, the best regeneration energy calculated was 2.5 GJ/tCO2 for an ideal operation with RITE-B.
texts.text: The RITE solvents were evaluated at the 1tCO2/d test plant. Regeneration energies were 3.3 GJ/tCO2 and 3.1 GJ/tCO2 for RITEA and RITE-B, respectively. Furthermore, after optimization for the RITE solvents, the best regeneration energy calculated was 2.5 GJ/tCO2 for an ideal operation with RITE-B.
